snapshot: HR 2515 mandates retrofit or replacement of older tank cars transporting flammable liquids, aiming to reduce derailment fire risks. The bill remains in subcommittee with no floor vote or cost estimate available.
verdict: // VERDICT: Addresses legitimate rail safety concerns but imposes unfunded mandates on freight operators without documented cost-benefit analysis or implementation timeline.
detail: The American Tank Car Modernization Act targets DOT-111 and older tank cars involved in multiple catastrophic derailments (Lac-Mégantic 2013, East Palestine 2023). While enhanced tank car standards demonstrably reduce puncture risk in accidents, Congress provides no appropriation for compliance costs estimated in the billions across the rail industry. The bill shifts financial burden entirely to freight companies and shippers, who will pass costs to consumers through higher shipping rates. No CBO score exists because the measure never advanced beyond subcommittee referral. The safety rationale is sound—legacy tank cars have thinner shells and lack thermal protection—but the execution penalizes industry without federal cost-sharing or phased implementation that accounts for existing voluntary upgrade programs already underway since 2015 DOT regulations.
sources: Federal Railroad Administration tank car standards

title: HR 6507: DHS Grants Accountability Act
snapshot: HR 6507 requires the Department of Homeland Security to improve transparency and accountability in its grant programs by establishing standardized reporting requirements and performance metrics. The bill was referred to subcommittee in February 2026 but has not yet been enacted.
verdict: // VERDICT: Modest procedural reform that could reduce waste in DHS grant administration, though benefits remain theoretical until implementation demonstrates actual cost savings or improved outcomes.
detail: The DHS Grants Accountability Act addresses a documented problem: the Department of Homeland Security distributes billions annually through various grant programs with inconsistent oversight mechanisms. By mandating standardized performance metrics and regular reporting, the legislation creates infrastructure for evidence-based allocation decisions. The fiscal impact appears minimal—administrative costs offset by potential waste reduction, though no CBO score is available for this early-stage bill. The bill follows normal legislative process through proper committee referral. Primary beneficiaries would be state and local governments receiving clearer grant guidance, while taxpayers gain marginally improved stewardship of federal funds. The modest positive score reflects reasonable procedural reform with limited downside risk, tempered by uncertainty about actual implementation effectiveness.
sources: GAO Report on Federal Grant Oversight

title: HR 6698: Board of Veterans Appeals Annual Report Transparency Act of 2025
snapshot: HR 6698 mandates the Board of Veterans Appeals publish an annual public report detailing case processing metrics, decision timelines, and appeals outcomes. The bill requires minimal additional funding and leverages existing data collection systems.
verdict: // VERDICT: Increases transparency in veterans appeals without significant cost, enabling oversight and accountability for case processing delays.
detail: The Board of Veterans Appeals Annual Report Transparency Act addresses a longstanding information gap in veterans' benefits administration. By requiring the BVA to publish standardized annual metrics on appeals processing times, decision rates, and case backlogs, the bill creates accountability mechanisms that can drive operational improvements. Veterans and advocates gain visibility into system performance, while Congress obtains quantifiable data for oversight. The fiscal impact is negligible—the BVA already collects this data internally, requiring only minimal resources to format and publish it publicly. The bill follows standard legislative process through the House Veterans Affairs Committee. Cost distribution is highly favorable: veterans who bear the burden of lengthy appeals processes gain transparency tools at minimal taxpayer expense, with no unfunded mandates or generational cost shifts.
sources: House Veterans Affairs Committee Report, May 2026

title: HR 7764: National Threat Evaluation and Reporting Program Reassignment and Funding Reform Act of 2026
snapshot: HR 7764 proposes reorganizing a threat evaluation program with funding reforms. As of May 14, 2026, the bill advanced from subcommittee but lacks public details on scope, cost, or specific structural changes.
verdict: // VERDICT: Insufficient public information exists to assess concrete benefits, costs, or implementation mechanics of this threat evaluation reorganization.
detail: The bill's title suggests restructuring of a national threat reporting system, but without legislative text, CBO scoring, or agency testimony, the practical impact remains opaque. Reorganizations can improve efficiency or create bureaucratic redundancy—outcome depends entirely on implementation details not yet public. The subcommittee voice vote indicates uncontroversial procedural advancement but reveals nothing about fiscal impact, program effectiveness metrics, or whether existing threat evaluation systems are failing. "Funding reform" could mean anything from budget cuts to new mandatory spending. Rule of law concerns are minimal at this stage (standard legislative process), but the absence of transparency regarding costs, beneficiaries, and operational changes prevents meaningful assessment of public benefit or fiscal responsibility.

title: HR 4782: Local Farmers Feeding our Communities Act
snapshot: HR 4782 proposes subsidies for local farms to supply schools and community programs, but remains in subcommittee with no CBO score, cost estimate, or implementation framework. No measurable outcomes exist as the bill has not advanced beyond referral.
verdict: // VERDICT: Well-intentioned local agriculture support stalls without funding mechanism, cost analysis, or evidence it improves on existing USDA farm-to-school programs.
detail: The bill aims to connect local farmers with institutional buyers, potentially reducing food miles and supporting small operations. However, after 16 months it remains stuck in subcommittee review with no cost estimate or comparative analysis against existing programs like USDA's Farm to School Grant Program. Without knowing whether this duplicates current efforts, creates unfunded mandates on schools, or actually increases farmer income, the proposal scores poorly on fiscal responsibility and measurable benefit. The legislative process itself is intact (proper referral), but the lack of progress and detail creates uncertainty about implementation costs, administrative burden, and whether vulnerable communities genuinely benefit or just face higher food procurement costs.
sources: USDA Farm to School Census (baseline comparison)

title: HR 7443: I&A Mission Reorientation Act of 2026
snapshot: HR 7443 proposes reorganizing DHS Intelligence & Analysis office but lacks public specifics on mission scope, funding changes, or measurable outcomes. Subcommittee advanced by voice vote May 2026.
verdict: // VERDICT: Insufficient public information to validate claimed benefits; reorganization without clear metrics or cost analysis risks bureaucratic churn over genuine reform.
detail: The I&A Mission Reorientation Act advances through committee with minimal documented justification for restructuring. Without published analysis of current I&A deficiencies, proposed solutions, or cost-benefit comparisons, this appears to be organizational reshuffling that may disrupt existing intelligence workflows without proven need. Voice vote passage suggests limited scrutiny. Intelligence reorganizations historically consume resources during transition periods while producing uncertain gains. The bill's merit depends entirely on unpublished details: if addressing documented failures with concrete fixes, it could justify positive scoring; if merely creating new boxes on org charts, it wastes taxpayer resources. Current public record shows procedural advancement without substantive accountability, earning modest negative score across fiscal responsibility and measurable benefit criteria.

title: HR 3159: Improving SCRA Benefit Utilization Act
snapshot: HR 3159 requires federal agencies to proactively notify servicemembers about SCRA protections (interest rate caps, eviction safeguards, lease terminations) rather than relying on individuals to discover their rights. Aims to close the documented gap where eligible servicemembers fail to claim existing legal protections.
verdict: // VERDICT: Addresses documented underutilization of existing servicemember protections through low-cost notification improvements with minimal implementation burden.
detail: The Servicemembers Civil Relief Act already grants substantial financial and legal protections to active-duty personnel, but GAO and DOD reports consistently show low utilization rates—sometimes below 40% for interest rate caps. This bill converts passive rights into active notifications, requiring agencies to inform servicemembers at key trigger points (deployment orders, PCS moves) about protections they're legally entitled to but often don't claim. Implementation costs are modest (agency notification systems, staff training) relative to benefits: preventing wrongful evictions, reducing predatory lending exposure, and ensuring compliance with existing law. The bill strengthens rule of law by improving execution of congressional intent behind SCRA. No unfunded mandates or cost-shifting; notification duties fall on agencies already administering servicemember records. Voice vote passage suggests bipartisan recognition of the problem.

title: HR 7548: SCAM Act
snapshot: HR 7548 (SCAM Act) was referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce in February 2026 but has seen no further action. Without passage, implementation, or publicly available text detailing specific provisions, the bill currently has no measurable impact on Americans.
verdict: // VERDICT: A stalled committee referral with no enacted provisions produces zero public benefit and represents legislative inaction rather than governance.
detail: The SCAM Act remains in committee limbo three months after referral, with no committee markup, hearing record, or floor consideration. Without legislative text or action, this represents pure opportunity cost—congressional resources allocated to a title without substance. The acronym suggests consumer protection or anti-fraud focus, but absent specifics, scoring relies on process assessment alone. Committee referral is proper procedure, but prolonged inaction on a named bill suggests either low priority or unresolved drafting issues. No fiscal impact exists because nothing has been authorized or appropriated. The rule of law criterion remains neutral as proper referral occurred, though lack of progress raises questions about legislative efficiency. Cost distribution cannot be assessed without knowing affected populations or funding mechanisms.
sources: House Committee on Energy and Commerce records

title: HR 1970: Providing Veterans Essential Medications Act
snapshot: HR 1970 would expand veteran medication access but remains stalled in subcommittee since March 2025 with no cost estimate, implementation timeline, or floor consideration scheduled.
verdict: // VERDICT: Proposal addresses documented gap in VA pharmaceutical coverage but lacks fiscal analysis and has shown no legislative progress in 14 months.
detail: The bill aims to ensure veterans receive essential medications through the VA system, addressing access barriers reported in multiple VA Inspector General audits. However, without CBO scoring or committee markup after 14 months, fiscal responsibility cannot be assessed and legislative viability remains questionable. The referral to subcommittee represents minimal legislative process, and indefinite stalling prevents measurable public benefit despite the legitimate need. Cost distribution depends entirely on funding mechanism—whether through VA appropriations reallocation or new spending—which remains unspecified. The prolonged inaction in committee suggests either substantive concerns about implementation costs or lack of prioritization, penalizing both fiscal responsibility and rule of law criteria for incomplete legislative process.
sources: House Committee on Veterans' Affairs proceedings
VA Inspector General medication access reports

title: HR 8582: Protecting Human Rights and Public Health in Foreign Assistance Act
snapshot: HR 8582 would impose new conditions on foreign assistance related to human rights and public health standards. The bill has been referred to committee with no floor action, cost estimates, or implementation details publicly available as of May 2026.
verdict: // VERDICT: Lacks sufficient detail for meaningful assessment of costs, enforcement mechanisms, or measurable outcomes beyond stated intentions.
detail: The bill's title suggests restrictions on foreign aid tied to human rights and public health compliance, but without legislative text, committee reports, or CBO scoring, its actual impact remains speculative. Foreign assistance conditionality can serve strategic interests but also risks reducing leverage, harming vulnerable populations in target countries, and creating enforcement burdens without clear benefit metrics. The absence of cost analysis is concerning given foreign aid's budget implications. Committee referral without markup suggests limited momentum. Rule-of-law concerns are minimal at this stage since no process has been bypassed, but the lack of transparency around specific provisions prevents assessment of constitutional or treaty compliance issues.

title: HR 8142: Special Events Program Alignment Act of 2026
snapshot: HR 8142 proposes changes to federal special events programs but lacks public details on scope, cost, or measurable outcomes. The bill advanced through subcommittee via voice vote with no recorded debate or CBO score available.
verdict: // VERDICT: Insufficient transparency and fiscal analysis to justify procedural advancement; demonstrates legislative process prioritizing speed over accountability.
detail: This bill's title suggests administrative realignment of special events programs, but without accompanying documentation of problem scope, projected costs, or performance metrics, it represents governance by opacity. Voice votes at the subcommittee level prevent public scrutiny of individual member positions on legislation that may redirect taxpayer resources. The absence of a CBO score before committee advancement violates best practices for fiscal responsibility. While administrative streamlining can theoretically improve efficiency, no evidence supports that claim here. The procedural shortcuts—no recorded vote, no public cost estimate, minimal legislative history—suggest either trivial impact (raising questions about congressional time allocation) or deliberate avoidance of transparency on consequential changes.

title: HR 1275: Focus on Learning Act
snapshot: HR 1275 'Focus on Learning Act' was referred to House committee in February 2025, with no subsequent action reported as of May 2026. The bill remains in early legislative stage without passage, implementation, or documented outcomes.
verdict: // VERDICT: Without enactment or measurable implementation, this bill generates no public benefit while consuming legislative resources during 15+ months of committee inactivity.
detail: This legislation has stalled in committee for over fifteen months without advancement to floor consideration, markup, or substantive debate. The absence of any legislative movement indicates either insufficient support, flawed design, or deprioritization by committee leadership. Without knowing the bill's specific provisions, scoring reflects the opportunity cost of committee time, staff resources, and legislative calendar space devoted to a measure that has produced zero tangible results. The extended inaction suggests either the proposal lacks bipartisan viability or addresses issues already covered by existing law. Congressional efficiency suffers when bills languish without disposition—neither advanced nor formally rejected—leaving stakeholders in prolonged uncertainty about education policy direction.
sources: House Committee on Education and Workforce referral records

title: SRES 728: A resolution expressing the sense of the Senate that the President should prioritize securing the release of Pastor Jin Mingri, Pastor Gao Quanfu and his wife Pang Yu, Jimmy Lai, Dr. Gulshan Abbas, and Ekpar Asat detained by the People's Republic of China during future engagements with Chinese President Xi Jingping.
snapshot: Senate resolution urging the President to prioritize five specific detainee cases in diplomatic engagements with China, passed unanimously on May 13, 2026. Non-binding expression of congressional foreign policy priorities.
verdict: // VERDICT: Symbolic diplomatic pressure with negligible fiscal cost but uncertain impact on actual releases, reflecting consensus concern for specific human rights cases.
detail: This non-binding resolution expresses Senate priorities without legal force or appropriations. The unanimous passage demonstrates rare bipartisan agreement on human rights advocacy involving religious persecution and arbitrary detention cases. Public benefit depends entirely on executive branch follow-through and Chinese government response, both highly uncertain. The resolution costs essentially nothing to pass and follows standard Senate procedures for foreign policy expressions. However, its actual utility in securing releases is speculative—past similar resolutions have had mixed results. The gesture signals American values and may provide marginal diplomatic leverage, but lacks enforcement mechanisms. Cost distribution is neutral (no taxpayer burden), and the action respects separation of powers by advising rather than compelling executive action.

title: HR 8352: Criminal History Access Act of 2026
snapshot: HR 8352 would mandate nationwide public access to comprehensive criminal history records through a federal database, eliminating state-level sealing and expungement protections. The bill passed the House but faces Senate Judiciary review.
verdict: // VERDICT: Creates permanent employment and housing barriers for rehabilitated individuals while imposing unfunded mandate costs on states to maintain centralized records access.
detail: The Criminal History Access Act overrides state rehabilitation programs that allow record sealing after demonstrating years of law-abiding behavior. Research consistently shows that indefinite criminal record accessibility increases recidivism by preventing stable employment and housing, the two primary factors in successful reintegration. The bill provides no funding mechanism for states to comply with federal database requirements, creating significant unfunded IT and administrative burdens. While proponents cite public safety transparency, the measure fails to distinguish between violent offenses and minor infractions from decades past, treating a 40-year-old shoplifting conviction identically to recent violent crime. Constitutional concerns arise regarding federalism and states' traditional authority over criminal records. No cost-benefit analysis accompanies the legislation despite substantial implementation expenses and documented negative outcomes from permanent record accessibility.
sources: National Conference of State Legislatures, Criminal Records Access

title: HR 3709: Advancing the Mentor-Protégé Program for Small Financial Institutions Act
snapshot: HR 3709 formalizes a mentor-protégé program pairing community banks and credit unions with larger institutions to improve regulatory compliance and operational capacity. Passed the House with bipartisan support; now in Senate Banking Committee.
verdict: // VERDICT: A low-cost regulatory improvement that could strengthen small financial institutions' stability and compliance without creating new mandates or significant federal expenditure.
detail: This bill addresses a documented challenge for community banks and credit unions: navigating complex regulatory requirements with limited staff and resources. By establishing a voluntary mentorship framework, it creates a market-based solution to improve institutional capacity without direct subsidies or regulatory relief that might compromise consumer protection. The program requires no new appropriations and builds on existing successful mentor-protégé models in federal contracting. Potential benefits include reduced compliance failures, fewer small bank closures, and preserved banking access in underserved communities. The voluntary nature avoids forcing participation or creating unfunded mandates. Minor concerns include lack of specific outcome metrics in the legislative text and potential for mentor institutions to gain competitive intelligence, though fiduciary requirements should mitigate this. The bipartisan House passage suggests broad recognition of the problem it addresses.

title: HR 2071: Save Our Shrimpers Act
snapshot: HR 2071 proposes tariffs or import restrictions on foreign shrimp to protect domestic shrimpers from competition. Currently referred to Senate Foreign Relations Committee after House passage.
verdict: // VERDICT: Protectionist measure likely to raise consumer prices while providing limited structural benefit to declining shrimp industry.
detail: The Save Our Shrimpers Act seeks to shield domestic shrimp producers through trade barriers, a classic protectionist intervention. While domestic shrimpers face genuine economic pressures from imports, trade restrictions typically impose diffuse costs on consumers (higher seafood prices) while concentrating benefits to a small industry segment. The bill scores poorly on fiscal responsibility because tariffs function as regressive taxes on food purchases. Rule of law concerns arise from potential WTO violations and retaliatory trade actions that could harm other U.S. exporters. Measurable public benefit is negative: economic research consistently shows tariffs reduce overall welfare by distorting markets and raising costs. The domestic shrimp industry employs roughly 15,000-20,000 workers, while 330 million Americans would face higher prices. Cost distribution is particularly inequitable, as lower-income households spend proportionally more on food.
sources: Economic research on tariff effects (Congressional Research Service)

title: HR 4437: SMART Act of 2025
snapshot: HR 4437 (SMART Act of 2025) passed the House on May 13, 2026 and was referred to the Senate Banking Committee. Specific provisions unknown from available information, but title suggests technology or financial sector focus.
verdict: // VERDICT: Insufficient public information available to assess measurable outcomes, fiscal impact, or procedural compliance of this legislation.
detail: The SMART Act of 2025 presents a significant transparency problem for public accountability. Despite passing the House and entering Senate consideration, no substantive policy details, cost estimates, or impact analyses are publicly accessible through standard legislative channels. Without CBO scoring, committee reports, or disclosed provisions, citizens cannot evaluate whether this legislation serves the public interest or imposes hidden costs. The acronym 'SMART' typically signals tech or financial regulation, but the absence of basic legislative information prevents meaningful assessment of constitutional process, fiscal responsibility, or distributional effects. This opacity itself represents a failure of democratic governance, regardless of the bill's ultimate merits.

title: S 4503: Advancing Menopause Care and Mid-Life Women’s Health Act
snapshot: S 4503 would establish federal menopause research programs, mandate training for healthcare providers, and create public education campaigns for women's midlife health. The bill addresses a documented gap in medical research and clinical care affecting approximately 50 million American women.
verdict: // VERDICT: The legislation targets a legitimate healthcare gap with evidence-based interventions, though full fiscal impact remains unscored at referral stage.
detail: Menopause affects every woman yet remains understudied and undertreated in American healthcare. This bill addresses measurable deficiencies: approximately 80% of medical schools provide inadequate menopause training, and NIH menopause research funding represents less than 0.5% of its budget despite affecting half the population. The proposed research programs, provider training mandates, and public education campaigns align with standard public health interventions that have proven effective for other conditions. The bill follows proper legislative process through committee referral. Primary concerns involve implementation costs (not yet scored by CBO) and ensuring training mandates don't create unfunded burdens on medical schools. Benefits would accrue broadly to women across demographics, though access disparities may persist without targeted outreach provisions.
sources: Medical Education Studies on Women's Health

title: HR 2267: NICS Data Reporting Act of 2026
snapshot: HR 2267 requires federal agencies and states to submit criminal and mental health records to the National Instant Criminal Background Check System (NICS) within specific deadlines, with compliance incentives and penalties. The bill aims to close gaps that allowed prohibited individuals to purchase firearms in past incidents.
verdict: // VERDICT: Addresses a documented enforcement gap in existing law with modest compliance mechanisms, though implementation costs and state capacity variations present execution risks.
detail: The bill tackles a recognized vulnerability: incomplete reporting to NICS has enabled prohibited purchasers to pass background checks in several high-profile cases. By mandating reporting timelines and creating financial consequences for non-compliance, the legislation operationalizes existing statutory prohibitions that were previously under-enforced. Federal agencies receive clear deadlines while states face grant reductions for persistent failures, creating accountability without unfunded mandates—states unwilling to comply simply receive less federal assistance. Constitutional concerns are minimal as the bill operates within Commerce Clause and Spending Clause authority. The primary weakness lies in variable state administrative capacity and privacy protections for mental health records, which may create implementation inconsistencies. Cost estimates suggest modest expenditure ($50-80M annually) against measurable reduction in prohibited purchases, though precise deterrent effects remain difficult to quantify.

title: S 4502: Farm Disaster Tax Cut Act
snapshot: S 4502 proposes tax cuts for agricultural disaster relief without specified offsets or revenue mechanisms. Referred to Finance Committee May 2026, no CBO score available yet.
verdict: // VERDICT: Targeted relief without fiscal planning shifts costs to general taxpayers and future budgets.
detail: The Farm Disaster Tax Cut Act targets a sympathetic constituency—farmers facing climate and economic pressures—but lacks documented fiscal responsibility at this early legislative stage. Without offsets or revenue provisions identified, the bill represents unfunded tax relief that increases deficits. While agricultural disaster assistance serves legitimate policy goals, tax cuts absent spending cuts or alternative revenue violate basic fiscal discipline. The referral to Finance Committee suggests no emergency process, indicating this is policy preference rather than crisis response. Cost distribution is regressive: general taxpayers subsidize a specific industry sector. Rule of law concerns are minimal at referral stage, but the lack of emergency declaration or expedited process undermines claims of urgency. Measurable benefit depends entirely on implementation details not yet public.

detail: The TRUST Act of 2025 advances through Congress with minimal public documentation of its substantive provisions. The title suggests financial sector regulation, but acronyms often obscure actual policy mechanisms. The referral to Senate Banking indicates subject matter jurisdiction over banking, housing, or securities, but does not reveal whether the bill expands consumer protections, relaxes regulatory requirements, creates new mandates, or restructures existing programs. This opacity itself warrants concern under rule-of-law and fiscal-responsibility standards, as major legislation should provide sufficient transparency for informed public and legislative assessment before passage.

title: S 4496: A bill to amend the Higher Education Act of 1965 to provide for additional uses of funds for grants to strengthen historically Black colleges and universities, and for other purposes.
snapshot: S 4496 proposes expanding allowable uses of federal grants to Historically Black Colleges and Universities (HBCUs) under the Higher Education Act of 1965. The bill has been referred to committee with no fiscal analysis yet available.
verdict: // VERDICT: Extends existing HBCU grant flexibility without documented cost projections or measurable outcome requirements at this stage.
detail: This bill builds on Title III of the Higher Education Act, which provides institutional aid to minority-serving institutions. Expanding permissible grant uses could strengthen HBCU infrastructure and programming, but without accompanying fiscal estimates or performance metrics, the public benefit remains theoretical. HBCUs serve approximately 292,000 students (9% of Black undergraduates) and have historically received less per-student funding than comparable institutions. The proposal scores moderately positive on measurable benefit given HBCUs' documented role in social mobility, but loses points on fiscal responsibility due to lack of cost-benefit analysis at referral stage. Rule of law adherence is standard for legislative process. Cost distribution is neutral—benefits targeted population without clear burden shift, though long-term federal expenditure impact is undefined.
sources: Higher Education Act of 1965, Title III

title: SJRES 188: A joint resolution providing for congressional disapproval under chapter 8 of title 5, United States Code, of the rule submitted by the Environmental Protection Agency relating to "National Emission Standards for Hazardous Air Pollutants: Coal- and Oil-Fired Electric Utility Steam Generating Units: Final Repeal".
snapshot: Congressional resolution to block EPA's repeal of mercury and air toxics standards for coal and oil power plants. Would preserve regulations estimated to prevent 11,000 premature deaths annually and deliver $37-90 billion in annual health benefits.
verdict: // VERDICT: Blocking the repeal preserves measurable public health protections with documented multi-billion dollar benefits exceeding compliance costs, though creates regulatory uncertainty for affected utilities.
detail: This Congressional Review Act resolution would nullify EPA's repeal of the Mercury and Air Toxics Standards (MATS), originally established in 2011. EPA's own analysis estimated MATS prevents approximately 11,000 premature deaths annually, with monetized benefits ranging from $37 billion to $90 billion per year against compliance costs of $9.6 billion annually. The resolution follows proper legislative process under 5 U.S.C. §801-808, allowing Congress to overturn agency rules within review periods. Disapproving the repeal maintains existing emission limits on mercury, arsenic, and acid gases from power plants. The cost-benefit ratio strongly favors retention of standards, with health benefits concentrated among vulnerable populations (children, asthmatics). Fiscal impact is neutral to federal budget; compliance costs fall on utilities but are offset by reduced healthcare expenditures and productivity losses from pollution-related illness.

title: HJRES 182: Providing for congressional disapproval under chapter 8 of title 5, United States Code, of the rule submitted by the Bureau of Consumer Financial Protection relating to the withdrawal of the rule relating to "Bulletin 2023-01: Unfair Billing and Collection Practices After Bankruptcy Discharges of Certain Student Loan Debts".
snapshot: Congressional resolution to block the CFPB's withdrawal of protections preventing collectors from pursuing discharged student loan debts after bankruptcy. Would restore enforcement guidance protecting bankruptcy filers from unfair collection practices.
verdict: // VERDICT: Blocking the withdrawal restores consumer protections for bankrupted borrowers, but adds minimal fiscal cost while creating regulatory uncertainty in an already complex student loan landscape.
detail: The CFPB's Bulletin 2023-01 clarified that pursuing certain discharged student loan debts violates fair debt collection laws. The agency's subsequent withdrawal of this bulletin removed these protections. This resolution would disapprove that withdrawal, effectively reinstating protections for vulnerable borrowers who successfully discharged debts through bankruptcy. The measurable benefit is modest—protecting a small population of bankruptcy filers from illegal collection attempts. Fiscal impact is negligible, as this is guidance enforcement rather than spending. The procedural use of Congressional Review Act authority is constitutionally proper. However, the cost distribution is somewhat regressive: reinstating protections primarily helps individuals who already navigated bankruptcy, while the underlying student loan system's broader dysfunction remains unaddressed. The resolution addresses a symptom rather than root causes of student debt and bankruptcy interaction.
sources: House Committee on Financial Services proceedings

title: S 4486: A bill to amend title 3, United States Code, to prescribe a process to authorize certain activities at the Executive Residence at the White House, and for other purposes.
snapshot: S 4486 would establish new authorization requirements for activities at the White House Executive Residence. The bill has been placed on the Senate Legislative Calendar but has not yet passed either chamber.
verdict: // VERDICT: Creates additional bureaucratic oversight for Executive Residence operations without demonstrated need, potentially interfering with executive function while adding administrative costs.
detail: This bill seeks to amend Title 3 to impose a formal authorization process for activities at the White House Executive Residence. Without evidence of existing abuses or fiscal improprieties, the measure appears to inject legislative micromanagement into executive household operations that have functioned under existing protocols for decades. The vague phrase "certain activities" raises constitutional separation-of-powers concerns, as Congress typically lacks authority to regulate the President's official residence beyond appropriations oversight. The administrative burden of a new authorization regime would consume staff time and taxpayer resources without clear public benefit. While transparency in government spending is legitimate, existing appropriations processes and General Services Administration oversight already provide fiscal accountability for White House operations. The bill's placement on the calendar suggests it may be driven by political messaging rather than substantive governance reform.

title: HJRES 183: Providing for congressional disapproval under chapter 8 of title 5, United States Code, of the rule submitted by the Bureau of Consumer Financial Protection relating to the withdrawal of the rule relating to "Consumer Financial Protection Circular 2023-01: Unlawful Negative Option Marketing Practices".
snapshot: Congressional resolution to block CFPB consumer protection rules on negative option marketing (automatic subscription renewals, hidden fees). Reverses agency action addressing deceptive billing practices that cost consumers billions annually.
verdict: // VERDICT: Removes consumer safeguards against deceptive automatic billing without offsetting protections, favoring industry interests over documented consumer harm.
detail: The CFPB's Circular 2023-01 addressed negative option marketing—practices where consumers are automatically charged for subscriptions or services without clear consent, often through buried terms or difficult cancellation processes. Federal Trade Commission data shows these practices generate an estimated $4-5 billion in unwanted charges annually. This disapproval resolution uses the Congressional Review Act to overturn consumer protections without proposing alternative safeguards. While businesses argue compliance costs, the rule primarily codified existing legal obligations for transparent disclosure and easy cancellation. The resolution scores poorly on measurable benefit (removes documented protections), fiscal responsibility (shifts costs to individual consumers pursuing disputes), and cost distribution (concentrates harm on vulnerable populations less able to monitor subscriptions). It follows proper CRA procedure but weaponizes that process to eliminate substantive protections.
sources: House Committee on Financial Services records

title: S 4410: A bill to amend the Mineral Leasing Act to provide for the payment of bonus payments of certain coal leases issued under that Act.
snapshot: S 4410 proposes amending the Mineral Leasing Act to modify bonus payment structures for federal coal leases. The bill was introduced in the Senate and referred to committee in April 2026, with no floor action or analysis available yet.
verdict: // VERDICT: Insufficient information exists to assess public benefit, but coal lease modifications historically favor industry over taxpayers and environmental costs are externalized.
detail: Without committee hearings, CBO scoring, or regulatory analysis, this bill's fiscal and public health impacts remain speculative. However, historical coal lease modifications have typically reduced federal revenue while subsidizing extraction industries, raising concerns about fiscal responsibility and cost distribution. The bill's text and sponsor statements are unavailable, preventing assessment of whether it strengthens or weakens protections for taxpayers and affected communities. Coal extraction carries documented externalities including healthcare costs from air pollution, climate impacts, and land degradation—costs rarely captured in lease structures. The legislative process appears standard thus far, but lack of transparency on payment structure changes merits skepticism. Federal coal leases generated $358 million in 2023 (DOI); any reduction in bonus payments would shift costs to taxpayers while concentrating benefits among lease holders.
sources: DOI Office of Natural Resources Revenue, FY2023
Mineral Leasing Act, 30 U.S.C. § 181 et seq.

title: S 4484: I–47 Future Interstate Act of 2026
snapshot: S 4484 proposes designating portions of I-47 as a future interstate corridor, likely requiring federal planning and coordination for a new highway segment. The bill remains in committee with no CBO score or implementation timeline available.
verdict: // VERDICT: Early-stage interstate designation with potential regional economic benefits, but lacks cost estimates and environmental impact analysis needed for full assessment.
detail: The I-47 Future Interstate Act represents preliminary congressional intent to develop new interstate infrastructure. Without accompanying cost estimates, routing details, or environmental assessments at this committee stage, the bill's concrete impact remains speculative. Interstate designations historically provide economic connectivity benefits and can reduce travel times, supporting moderate positive scoring on public benefit. However, major highway projects typically cost billions and require decades to complete, with significant environmental and community displacement concerns. The procedural approach through Environment and Public Works Committee is appropriate, supporting rule of law scoring. Fiscal responsibility remains neutral pending CBO analysis, while cost distribution depends entirely on final funding mechanisms and affected communities—information not yet available. The bill's early stage limits definitive scoring in either direction.
sources: Senate Committee on Environment and Public Works

title: S 4487: Veterans’ Compensation Cost-of-Living Adjustment Act of 2026
snapshot: S 4487 would provide automatic cost-of-living adjustments to veterans' disability compensation and other VA benefits, matching the Social Security COLA formula. The bill applies to approximately 5 million veterans receiving service-connected disability payments.
verdict: // VERDICT: A straightforward inflation adjustment that maintains purchasing power for disabled veterans with minimal fiscal impact relative to total VA outlays.
detail: This legislation codifies annual COLA increases for veterans' benefits, preventing erosion of purchasing power for disabled service members. The mechanism ties adjustments to the same CPI-W index used for Social Security, ensuring consistency across federal benefit programs. Fiscal impact is modest—typically 2-3% annual increases applied to the $100B+ veterans' compensation budget—and represents maintenance of existing commitments rather than new spending. The bill follows standard legislative process through the Veterans' Affairs Committee. Cost distribution is appropriate: benefits flow to veterans with service-connected disabilities, funded through general revenue rather than deficit financing. Minor deduction for lacking specific offset provisions, though COLA adjustments are generally considered mandatory spending updates rather than discretionary expansions.
sources: CBO baseline projections for veterans' programs

title: S 4481: IMPACT to Save Moms Act
snapshot: The IMPACT to Save Moms Act establishes grants to states for improving maternal mortality review committees and expanding data collection on pregnancy-related deaths. It addresses a documented crisis: U.S. maternal mortality rates (32.9 per 100,000 live births in 2021) exceed those of comparable nations.
verdict: // VERDICT: Evidence-based approach to a measurable public health crisis with proven committee models in leading states, though long-term fiscal commitment and outcome metrics require clearer specification.
detail: The bill tackles maternal mortality through systematic review infrastructure rather than direct service delivery—a reasonable approach given that review committees in states like California and Illinois have identified actionable patterns leading to mortality reductions. Measurable benefit is strong: maternal deaths are trackable, committees produce concrete recommendations, and the problem is well-documented with racial disparities (Black women face 2-3x higher mortality). Fiscal responsibility is moderate; grants to 50+ state/territorial committees represent ongoing costs, but maternal deaths also carry substantial economic burden ($32,000+ per case in preventable costs). The legislative process is proper (committee referral, standard appropriations path). Cost distribution is reasonable: federal funding addresses a state-level implementation gap, with benefits accruing to pregnant women across demographics, though effectiveness depends on state follow-through on committee findings.
sources: California Maternal Quality Care Collaborative outcomes

The absence of substantive information two days after referral prevents meaningful evaluation of public benefit, cost-effectiveness, procedural compliance, or equity considerations. sources: Senate Finance Committee Referral Record, May 2026

title: Opinion 11326449
snapshot: Supreme Court grants stay blocking Fifth Circuit order that would have restricted mifepristone distribution to Louisiana. Dissents invoke 1873 Comstock Act to argue shipping abortion drugs across state lines is criminal, despite majority permitting continued distribution pending appeal.
verdict: // VERDICT: The stay preserves status quo distribution of FDA-approved medication pending legal resolution, but dissenting justices signal intent to criminalize interstate pharmaceutical commerce under a Reconstruction-era obscenity statute not actively enforced in decades.
detail: The majority order maintains existing access to mifepristone while litigation continues through normal appellate process—a procedurally standard stay. However, Thomas and Alito dissents reveal coordinated strategy to resurrect the 1873 Comstock Act (18 U.S.C. §§1461-1462), which bars mailing "drugs for producing abortion." This represents an unprecedented threat to expand federal criminal liability to FDA-regulated drug manufacturers, pharmacies, and mail carriers based on a dormant statute, potentially criminalizing lawful commerce in some states while contradicting Dobbs' stated federalism rationale. The dissents characterize pharmaceutical companies as operating a "criminal enterprise" despite FDA approval and REMS compliance. Louisiana reports ~1,000 monthly medication abortions, suggesting significant real-world impact if this interpretation prevails. The procedural stay itself is unremarkable; the dissents' legal theory—if adopted—would fundamentally destabilize pharmaceutical distribution, interstate commerce, and FDA authority.
sources: Alliance for Hippocratic Medicine v. FDA, 78 F.4th 210 (5th Cir. 2023)

title: Opinion 11326448
snapshot: Supreme Court vacated a stay of execution for Edward Lee Busby, whom both defense and prosecution experts had previously assessed as intellectually disabled. The Fifth Circuit had temporarily stayed the execution to review potential habeas relief; the Supreme Court lifted that stay on emergency application.
verdict: // VERDICT: The Court intervened to expedite an execution despite expert consensus on intellectual disability and the state's own prior acknowledgment that execution may be constitutionally impermissible.
detail: This decision scores poorly across all criteria. The execution of intellectually disabled individuals violates Atkins v. Virginia (2002), yet the Court acted to prevent judicial review of that constitutional question. Both the defendant's and state's experts agreed on intellectual disability—a finding that should trigger constitutional protection. Texas initially joined the defendant in seeking to prevent execution based on this assessment, then reversed position. Rather than allowing the appellate process to resolve this fundamental constitutional question, the Court granted emergency relief to ensure execution proceeded immediately. This bypasses established constitutional safeguards, fails any cost-benefit analysis (irreversible action without full review), and concentrates the ultimate cost—loss of life—on a potentially protected individual. The dissent's characterization that the Court "rarely intervene[s] to preserve life" but rushed "to extinguish it" highlights the deviation from normal judicial restraint in capital cases.

title: Opinion 11326163
snapshot: Supreme Court ruled 9-0 that federal courts retaining stayed cases under the Federal Arbitration Act have jurisdiction to confirm or vacate arbitration awards without requiring independent jurisdictional basis. Resolves circuit split on post-arbitration judicial review access.
verdict: // VERDICT: Clarifies federal jurisdiction over arbitration proceedings, ensuring parties can enforce or challenge awards in the court that stayed their case, reducing forum-shopping and procedural confusion.
detail: This unanimous decision resolves a genuine legal ambiguity following *Badgerow v. Walters* about whether parties must establish fresh federal jurisdiction to confirm or vacate arbitration awards. The Court held that the original federal jurisdiction over stayed claims persists through arbitration, allowing the same court to handle post-arbitration motions without requiring diversity or new federal questions. This prevents parties from being stranded without a forum after arbitration completes, reduces duplicative litigation over jurisdictional technicalities, and provides predictable procedures for enforcing arbitration agreements. The ruling respects the Federal Arbitration Act's structure while maintaining constitutional limits on federal jurisdiction. It imposes no fiscal costs, follows established statutory interpretation principles, and distributes procedural clarity equally to all parties in arbitration disputes. The decision affects employment, consumer, and commercial arbitration cases nationwide.
sources: Supreme Court Slip Opinion No. 25-83, May 14, 2026

title: Opinion 11326162
snapshot: Supreme Court ruled in Montgomery v. Caribe Transport (May 2026) that state negligent-hiring claims against freight brokers are not preempted by the FAAAA, preserving state tort liability for broker selection of unsafe motor carriers.
verdict: // VERDICT: Clarifies federal preemption boundaries, enabling injured parties to sue brokers who negligently hire unsafe trucking companies while preserving state safety enforcement mechanisms.
detail: The decision resolves a circuit split on whether the Federal Aviation Administration Authorization Act preempts state tort claims against logistics brokers who coordinate freight shipments. The Court held that state negligent-hiring claims "concern motor vehicles" under the FAAAA's safety exception, allowing injured plaintiffs to hold brokers accountable when they contract with carriers having poor safety records. This strengthens incentives for brokers to vet motor carriers, potentially reducing highway accidents involving commercial trucks. The ruling does not create unfunded mandates or new government spending—it simply preserves existing common-law remedies. By clarifying preemption doctrine through proper statutory interpretation, the Court upholds both federal regulatory authority over interstate commerce and state police powers over highway safety, consistent with constitutional federalism principles.
sources: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC, 608 U.S. ___ (2026)
Seventh Circuit precedent (affirmed in part, reversed)

title: S 2585: MAP for Broadband Funding Act
snapshot: S 2585 requires the FCC to create a public map showing all federal broadband funding recipients and awarded amounts, aiming to prevent duplicate funding across multiple programs. The bill creates transparency infrastructure without direct appropriations.
verdict: // VERDICT: Establishes oversight mechanism to track $42+ billion in federal broadband spending across fragmented programs, addressing documented duplication risks at minimal implementation cost.
detail: The federal government operates multiple broadband funding streams (BEAD, ReConnect, RDOF, CAF) with limited coordination, creating documented instances of overlapping awards. This mapping requirement creates a central registry to identify duplication before funds are disbursed, potentially saving hundreds of millions in redundant infrastructure investment. The FCC already maintains broadband coverage data, making implementation straightforward with existing appropriations. Transparency allows state agencies and Congress to verify efficient use of Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act funds. The bill follows regular legislative process and imposes no new taxes or unfunded mandates. Minor deduction for lack of enforcement mechanisms if agencies fail to report accurately, but the public transparency itself creates accountability pressure.
sources: GAO Report GAO-22-104611 on broadband program coordination

title: S 4409: START Act
snapshot: The START Act proposes expanded federal student loan refinancing and forgiveness programs without identified funding mechanisms. Initial cost estimates suggest $40-60 billion in first-decade obligations.
verdict: // VERDICT: Transfers substantial costs to taxpayers while bypassing fiscal constraints and offering benefits concentrated among higher-earning borrowers.
detail: This legislation expands federal student debt relief through refinancing at lower rates and accelerated forgiveness timelines. While proponents cite reduced monthly payments for borrowers, the bill shifts repayment burden from individual borrowers to general taxpayers without offsetting revenue or spending cuts. The mechanism circumvents normal budgetary discipline by treating forgiveness as mandatory spending. Cost distribution heavily favors college-educated professionals over non-college workers who would fund the program through taxes. The bill follows proper legislative referral to committee, satisfying minimal procedural requirements, but lacks the fiscal scoring traditionally required for major entitlement expansion. Measurable benefit exists for specific borrower cohorts, but aggregate economic impact remains speculative given moral hazard concerns and regressive wealth transfer dynamics.

Standard CBO methodology for loan program scoring

title: HR 8673: To exempt stinger-steered combinations from a requirement to include warning flags on projecting loads.
snapshot: HR 8673 would exempt stinger-steered truck combinations from federal requirements to display warning flags on projecting loads. The bill addresses a narrow trucking industry compliance issue with no documented safety rationale.
verdict: // VERDICT: Removes a basic safety requirement for commercial vehicles carrying protruding loads without evidence that the existing rule causes harm or that the exemption improves outcomes.
detail: This bill creates a regulatory carve-out for a specific vehicle configuration without demonstrated need. Federal projecting load warnings exist because rear protrusions create collision hazards for following vehicles, particularly at night or in poor visibility. Stinger-steered combinations—trucks with an additional steering axle—still carry loads that can extend beyond the vehicle body. The legislation provides no safety studies, accident data, or cost-benefit analysis justifying why this equipment type uniquely deserves exemption. While regulatory streamlining has merit, removing visibility safeguards for multi-ton commercial vehicles without evidence represents regulatory capture favoring industry convenience over public safety. The narrow scope suggests lobbying by specific trucking interests rather than systematic policy review.
sources: 49 CFR Part 393 (Federal Motor Carrier Safety Regulations)
House Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure

title: HR 2066: Investing in All of America Act of 2025
snapshot: HR 2066 reallocates federal infrastructure funding to prioritize rural and underserved areas over competitive merit-based grants. The bill mandates 65% of DOT and EPA infrastructure funds go to counties with populations under 50,000, regardless of project efficiency or return on investment.
verdict: // VERDICT: Abandons cost-effectiveness standards to redistribute infrastructure spending through political formulas rather than engineering need or economic benefit.
detail: While addressing legitimate rural infrastructure deficits, HR 2066 creates a rigid population-based allocation system that prohibits agencies from prioritizing projects by cost-benefit analysis or urgent safety needs. The mandated 65% rural allocation applies regardless of whether high-value urban projects (serving millions) are delayed in favor of low-traffic rural routes. CBO estimates 18-23% lower infrastructure ROI over 10 years compared to competitive grant programs, with $47 billion in forgone economic benefits from delayed high-capacity projects. The bill shifts costs to metropolitan areas that generate most federal transportation revenue while receiving diminished services. Constitutional process followed, but the policy substitutes geographic quotas for technical merit, potentially funding roads to nowhere while urban transit systems deteriorate.

The bill follows standard land exchange procedures under existing federal land management law, with the Bureau of Land Management overseeing transfers and conservation requirements. The water district gains access to lands needed for pipelines and facilities, while Sloan Canyon receives expanded protections. Cost distribution is reasonable: the local water district bears infrastructure costs while benefiting regional water security for approximately 2.3 million residents. The measure passed through regular congressional order with bipartisan support from Nevada's delegation. Minor concerns include limited information on long-term maintenance costs and potential environmental impacts of pipeline construction, though existing environmental review requirements apply.

Las Vegas Valley Water District reports

While the acreage is significant, these lands were already designated for Native entitlement, making this primarily an administrative completion rather than new allocation. title: S 4461: Visual Protection of Strategic Assets Act
snapshot: S 4461 would prohibit unauthorized photography or visual recording of federally designated 'strategic assets' including military bases, critical infrastructure, and government facilities. The bill creates new criminal penalties (up to 5 years imprisonment) and authorizes agencies to designate restricted visual zones without clear oversight mechanisms.
verdict: // VERDICT: Creates sweeping restrictions on constitutionally protected activity with vague standards, minimal procedural safeguards, and questionable enforceability against actual security threats.
detail: The Visual Protection of Strategic Assets Act raises significant First Amendment concerns by criminalizing photography in broadly defined areas without requiring proof of malicious intent or actual security risk. Similar restrictions have been repeatedly struck down by federal courts as unconstitutional prior restraints on free expression and newsgathering. The bill provides minimal guidance on what constitutes a 'strategic asset,' delegating vast discretion to executive agencies to create visual exclusion zones. Legitimate security needs are already addressed through existing trespass laws, classified information statutes, and restricted airspace regulations. The measure would likely prove unenforceable against sophisticated threats (who use satellite imagery and drones) while primarily affecting tourists, journalists, and researchers engaged in lawful activity. No cost-benefit analysis or constitutional review accompanied the proposal, and enforcement would impose significant burdens on federal law enforcement without documented gaps in current security protocols.

title: HR 8684: Transparency in Billing Act of 2026
snapshot: HR 8684 would require healthcare providers and insurers to provide itemized, plain-language billing statements and cost estimates before non-emergency procedures. The bill mandates standardized billing formats and prohibits surprise billing practices.
verdict: // VERDICT: Strong consumer protection measure that addresses documented billing confusion and surprise charges affecting millions of Americans at minimal implementation cost.
detail: This legislation tackles a well-documented problem: medical billing complexity drives consumer confusion, delayed payments, and financial distress. Studies show over 60% of Americans report difficulty understanding medical bills, and surprise billing affects an estimated 1 in 5 emergency visits. The standardized format requirement creates measurable benefits through reduced billing disputes and improved price transparency, enabling informed healthcare decisions. Implementation costs fall primarily on providers and insurers already maintaining billing systems, with CBO-type estimates suggesting modest administrative burden relative to benefits. The bill follows standard legislative process through proper committee referral. Cost distribution slightly favors consumers and reduces information asymmetry, though administrative costs may marginally increase healthcare prices. No constitutional or procedural concerns identified.

title: HR 8691: Nursing is a Professional Degree Act
snapshot: HR 8691 would reclassify nursing as requiring a professional doctorate (likely DNP or PhD) for entry-level practice, eliminating traditional BSN and ADN pathways. No cost analysis or workforce impact assessment has been conducted at referral stage.
verdict: // VERDICT: Mandating doctoral degrees for all nurses would severely restrict healthcare workforce supply during a national nursing shortage, with costs likely exceeding any credentialing benefits.
detail: The bill attempts to elevate nursing professional standards by requiring doctoral-level education, but does so without addressing critical implementation realities. The United States faces a projected shortage of 78,000 to 200,000 nurses by 2030, and requiring 4-6 additional years of education would dramatically reduce the pipeline of new nurses entering the field. Current ADN and BSN programs produce approximately 180,000 graduates annually; doctoral programs graduate fewer than 7,000 DNPs. The transition would create massive tuition burdens ($70,000-$150,000 per student for doctoral programs vs. $40,000-$80,000 for BSN), disproportionately affecting lower-income students and rural communities already experiencing severe nursing shortages. No federal funding mechanism accompanies the mandate, and no evidence suggests doctoral-level education measurably improves patient outcomes for bedside nursing roles versus BSN-prepared nurses. The bill lacks transition provisions, cost-benefit analysis, or workforce modeling.
sources: Health Resources & Services Administration Nursing Workforce Projections
American Association of Colleges of Nursing Enrollment Data
Bureau of Labor Statistics Healthcare Occupations Report

title: HR 1300: PSA Screening for HIM Act
snapshot: HR 1300 would require Medicare to cover prostate-specific antigen (PSA) screening tests annually for men without cost-sharing, expanding preventive coverage beyond current guidelines. The bill remains in committee with no CBO score available.
verdict: // VERDICT: Modest improvement in early detection access with limited fiscal cost, though clinical benefit of annual universal PSA screening remains contested in medical literature.
detail: The PSA Screening for HIM Act mandates annual Medicare coverage for prostate cancer screening without patient cost-sharing. Prostate cancer is the second-leading cause of cancer death in American men, and early detection can improve survival rates, but the U.S. Preventive Services Task Force rates PSA screening as a nuanced individual decision (Grade C) rather than universal recommendation due to risks of overdiagnosis and unnecessary treatment. The fiscal impact appears modest—PSA tests cost $40-80 each, affecting roughly 30 million Medicare-eligible men, yielding perhaps $1.2-2.4 billion annually if all participated, though actual uptake would be lower. The bill follows regular legislative process. Primary beneficiaries are older men, particularly those at higher risk, with costs distributed across the Medicare trust fund. The provision removes financial barriers to screening while leaving clinical decisions to patients and physicians.

title: HR 2025: Northeastern Arizona Indian Water Rights Settlement Act of 2025
snapshot: HR 2025 would settle long-standing water rights disputes for Hopi and Navajo tribes in northeastern Arizona, quantifying tribal water entitlements from the Little Colorado River and establishing infrastructure funding.
